text: “Everyone Knows AI” - Report about Attitudes towards Artificial Intelligence in Poland Based on 12 surveys performed by various Polish media outlets between 2017 and 2023 this report attempts to assess in a more systematic fashion how Poles currently understand and relate to artefacts, systems and phenomena classified under a label of ‘artificial intelligence’ (AI). These findings are derived from a systematic review of twelve public opinion surveys on attitudes towards AI, carried out in 2017-2023 by several of the most popular Polish media outlets, with a focus on gathering information necessary for a thematic analysis and revealing patterns and their possible evolution over time. Our primary goals consist in 1) evaluating how society thinks about the issue of AI, determining how it understands what exactly is labeled “AI” and tracing what ideas it substitutes in its place; 2) trying to assess experiences with the AI-related artefacts, their occurrence in everyday life, understanding of what AI technology is applied for and determining the threshold of acceptability when it comes to more unusual applications of contemporary computing, which have been assigned the term AI; 3) describing fears concerning unemployment due to increased effectiveness of computations as well as assessing societal concerns related to increased use of AI technology. Finally, based on the description of the spectrum of current themes connected with perceptions of AI, our fourth goal is identifying specific aspects that seem to be a source of mental discomfort caused by increased use of so-called AI-related solutions. For some of these aspects the cause of the observed discomfort will be explicitly mentioned, and although on the face of it it seems difficult to find the rationale behind current fears or misunderstanding related to intelligent ICT systems – often they amount to simply more science fiction associated with ICT than an evidence-based concern of meaningful social relevance, we hypothesise, that over the coming years, they may be replaced by totally different relations with the use of innovations assigned the label AI, for instance from seeing a depiction of self-driving (perhaps even piloted) Air cars in favourite films and TV serials to understanding – thanks to mass media – their potential replacement, as well as the realisation of other effects that intelligent programmes and connected applications will bring into the Polish everyday life and which are currently either as yet unimaginable, or insufficiently known.
